> I don't know why we are checking '0'

I'll answer this first - the first and second columns are the device major and minor numbers. See Documentation/iostats.txt in the kernel source tree for more details on the format of /proc/diskstats.

> Would it be accurate if we remove if data[1] == '0': and count all lines for all disks?

Nope, because the contents of this file isn't just physical drives. Take the case where you have "sda" which has a partition "sda1" and then LUKS on top of that as "dm-0" containing the filesystem. Without the check for the minor device number of 0, any data written to that filesystem would be counted 3X! Note that with the check for the minor number, it's still counted 2X, so it's still wrong.

The problem here is that this data is not just physical disks. These are block devices - they can be physical, logical, or even something like a loopback device.

Yeah, I agree. It's made a lot of things more verbose, especially sorting a list of tuples according to a custom key. It's one of the few changes I'd consider a straight-up regression from Python 2 to 3.  Still, Python 3.5 now has enough new features and enough library support that it's unequivocally the choice for new projects, and the "six" approach to developing has generally won over the 2to3 approach from what I've seen in libraries trying to support both versions.

As regards the naming, the `Visitor` API passes two arguments, which in my visitors I usually name `def visit_FOO(self, node, children):` 

I almost never care about `node` _unless_ I want `node.text`, otherwise, I unpack (and possibly deserialize) elements of the tuple of children. What do you think about changing the naming convention in this file to something like the following?
```python
def visit_parenthesized(self, node, parenthesized):
    """Treat a parenthesized subexpression as just its contents.

    Its position in the tree suffices to maintain its grouping semantics.

    """
    (left_paren, _1, expression, right_paren, _2) = parenthesized
    return expression
```

> Does that mean there's a missing unit test? `do_pickle(phasescreen_psf)`

This took a bit of tinkering to figure out.  We did have some `do_pickle(phasescreen_psf)`s, but none of them triggered an error for two reasons:

1) The existing ones were all for atmospheric phase screens that have an `rng` class member and so the eval(repr()) test gets skipped since we don't expect the BaseDeviates to pass an eval(repr()) roundtrip.
2) When I made a `do_pickle` on an `OpticalScreen` `do_pickle` test, the try/except block designed to catch unevaluatable reprs triggered, skipping the test.

So I rewrote the eval/repr roundtrip test, and got this error to trigger.  Doing that also brought up a bunch of other errors, though, so I went through and fixed all of them.

Quoted user names are rarely used as well, so clunkiness of expressing them in the YAML is not really an issue. The idea is to lowercase all user names by default, unless the name contains quotes, in which case it should be passed verbatim to the "CREATE USER". Just for clarity, let me summarize the current implementation:

- When creating a `Policy` instance from the resource returned by the `getIAMPolicy` API, it conflates uses with either the "legacy" `roles/owner` or and the "real" `roles/pubsub.admin` into its `owners` set.   Likewise for editors and viewers.  The reason for this implementation is that early on (two years back) there were users who had some topics (maybe subscriptions) with the legacy roles set, but the newer ones were entirely equivalent (i.e., mapped onto the same permissions relevant for topics/subscriptioions).

- When sending the resource *back* to the `setIAMPolicy` API, the policy discards the legacy roles because,  as @elibixby points out, they are not allowed to be set for pubsub topics or subscriptions (they were not allowed to be set even then).

So, nobody has been relying on setting legacy roles since before we added IAM support to pubsub:  it was never possible at all.  Silently converting existing legacy roles to the acceptable equivalents seemed like a good tradeoff at the time.  The other choice would have been to let the `setIAMPolicy` blow up if the user didn't clean out / replace the legacy roles.

What is the type of `self.data_str`?

* If it's `bytes` (`data_str = b'test'`), then `str(data_str)` gives "b'test'" on python 3
* If it's `unicode` (`data_str = u'snowman: \u2603'`), then `str(data_str)` raises a `UnicodeEncodeError`

I think what you actually want here is:

```
unicode = type(u'')   # or from six import unicode
s = self.data_str
if isinstance(s, unicode):
    s = s.encode('utf-8')
```

This was added a long time for the (apparently not uncommon) case where you are reading a collection of CSV files (`tc.SFrame.read_csv('blah/*.csv')`), where each CSV file has a header, AND some files are 0 bytes long. Not skipping the 0 byte files cause the CSV header parse to fail resulting in an unexpected parse failure. 

We could catch the parse failure, but it can be hard to differentiate between a malformed CSV file (in which case we should fail), and this very particular use case (in which case we should just skip and continue). Hence it was easier to just check for the 0 byte case specifically before hand.

The API surrounding parsing comments here is pretty messy, and (though I haven't tested it) I'm fairly certain that it doesn't work in 100% of cases (example: in order to implement a proper multiline comment parser for Python, what you actually need is a string parser, and this code recognizes no escape sequences).  

In order to make everyone's lives easier what you instead want is the function:

    def _chomp_comment(language, code):
         """Given a block of code in the source language, return all the code after an initial comment if there is one."""
        ...

Then the implementation of `_chomp_comment` can simply iterate through all the characters in the code block and return a new string after all the comments/whitespace at the beginning.  This is more flexible and allows you to actually capture all the corner cases.

It's to avoid repeatedly appending to the end of a `deque` (which would be accidentally quadratic if this were an `ArrayDeque`, but is not technically quadratic because CPython's `deque` implementation uses a blocked-linked-list). (Analogously, `n = int(1e6); x = range(n); for i in range(n, 2 * n): x.append(i)` vs `x = range(n); x.extend(range(n, 2 * n))`: the former is not quadratic because of amortized resizing, but certainly does have the potential to be noticeably slower due to intermediate resizes.)

This issue would be likely to affect any plugin, not just histogram/images.  I'll go into specifics below, but the TL;DR is that my point is that using the step column to look for NULL is nearly always at least as good as using dtype, and likely much more efficient in many queries.  So unless there's some really good reason to make dtype the indicator of placeholder-ness, we should use step.

SQL DB indexes pretty much exist to support WHERE clauses, so it's not really the case that WHERE clauses imply you need to do a scan... in many cases it implies the opposite.  When you use a WHERE clause filter on an indexed column where the WHERE condition is indexable (i.e. it's a pure comparison), it's more-or-less telling SQLite that it can use that index to skip ahead to values matching the filter condition instead of scanning the table.  See this section of the SQLite query optimizer: https://sqlite.org/optoverview.html#where_clause

Some specifics:

1. For the case above, SQLite can't use the TensorSeriesStepIndex at all if you don't filter for step IS NOT NULL.  That means that it actually has to scan the entire Tensors table, which is even worse than I had originally said above (it's O(# of series X max steps per series)).  With step IS NOT NULL, it can seek directly to the part of the TensorSeriesStepIndex matching the series ID provided, so it only has to look at the O(# of steps) rows for that specific series to determine the min/max.

I had thought in my above analysis that SQLite would be smart enough to use the TensorSeriesStepIndex within the series-specific range to look up the min/max in O(log # of steps) or O(1) time, but based on the full EXPLAIN results for the query, it's not that smart - it will only do that optimization when the MIN/MAX argument is the leftmost column of an index: https://sqlite.org/optoverview.html#minmax  But it's still a huge speedup to only inspect the data for a single series in this query versus all series in the Tensors table.

2. For the pr curves plugin query (and likely similar for scalars), it also makes a big difference.  The respective query plans for the PR curves query are as follows:

With `dtype IS NOT NULL`:
```text
0|0|0|SCAN TABLE Tensors
0|1|1|SEARCH TABLE Tags USING INDEX TagIdIndex (tag_id=?)
0|2|2|SEARCH TABLE Runs USING INDEX RunIdIndex (run_id=?)
```

With `step IS NOT NULL`:
```text
0|0|1|SCAN TABLE Tags USING INDEX TagRunNameIndex
0|1|2|SEARCH TABLE Runs USING INDEX RunIdIndex (run_id=?)
0|2|0|SEARCH TABLE Tensors USING INDEX TensorSeriesStepIndex (series=?)
```

Again, we can only use TensorSeriesStepIndex if we filter out null steps.  SQLite joins are implemented as nested for loops, so in the first case, we are literally looping over the entire tensors table again (even though the query is targeting a specific set of runs and a specific single tag).  For each Tensor we have to join it to its tag and run in order to check if they match the tag and run name.  In the second case, we instead scan TagRunNameIndex which is O(# of unique run-tag combinations), which reduces the scanned surface by O(# of steps per tag) like above.  We then do an index lookup into Runs to check the run_name, and then use the tag_id to index into Tensors to scan data for just that tag.

This could actually be improved further by adding `AND experiment_id IS NULL` to the query, which then lets us use RunNameIndex to look up the run_id, which in turns lets us use TagRunNameIndex to do an index lookup into Tags instead of a scan.  The resulting query plan is:

```text
0|0|0|SEARCH TABLE Runs USING INDEX RunNameIndex (experiment_id=? AND run_name=?)
0|1|1|SEARCH TABLE Tags USING INDEX TagRunNameIndex (run_id=? AND tag_name=?)
0|2|2|SEARCH TABLE Tensors USING INDEX TensorSeriesStepIndex (series=?)
```

This is another drastic improvement since we now are doing work per run queried that's O( log( # of runs) + log(# of tags) + log(# of tensors) + # of tensors for this specific tag), so it scales linearly only with the actual data that we're returning instead of the full DB size.
